Outliers And Summaries

Goal

This notebook shows how to use visualization and summaries to find unusual values.

Outliers are not automatically errors. They are values worth investigating.

Analytic Questions

We will ask:

  • What does the distribution of daily energy intake look like in the teaching file?
  • Which values are unusually high by a simple IQR rule?
  • How do we visualize outliers without letting them dominate the whole analysis?

Load Data

nhanes_path <- "data/real/nhanes_2021_2023_analysis.csv"

nhanes <- read_csv(nhanes_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

nhanes |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 11,933
Columns: 57
$ respondent_id                 <dbl> 130378, 130379, 130380, 130381, 130382, …

$ day2_energy_kcal              <dbl> 1040, 2228, 1475, 1068, 1067, NA, NA, NA…

$ day2_total_sugars_g           <dbl> 0.41, 113.41, 107.18, 59.36, 55.13, NA, …

$ day2_sodium_mg                <dbl> 1660, 3438, 2979, 1711, 1770, NA, NA, NA…

$ adult_bp_category_simplified  <chr> "stage_2_range", "normal_range", "normal…

Prepare Dietary Data

diet <- nhanes |>
  filter(!is.na(mean_daily_energy_kcal))

diet |>
  select(respondent_id, age_years, gender, age_group, mean_daily_energy_kcal) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 5
  respondent_id age_years gender age_group mean_daily_energy_kcal
          <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>  <chr>                      <dbl>
1        130378        43 male   30-44                      1390 
2        130379        66 male   65-79                      2484.
3        130380        44 female 30-44                      1735 
4        130381         5 female 0-17                       1372 
5        130382         2 male   0-17                       1144.
6        130386        34 male   30-44                      2133

The dietary summaries are simple averages over available recall days. They are for exploratory teaching examples, not formal usual-intake estimation.

Plot The Distribution

p1 <- ggplot(
  diet,
  aes(x = mean_daily_energy_kcal)
)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 250)

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution Of Mean Daily Energy Intake",
    subtitle = "Simple mean across available dietary recall days",
    x = "Mean daily energy intake, kcal",
    y = "Number of participants"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3

Calculate A Simple Outlier Rule

Use the common 1.5 IQR rule as a screening tool.

energy_cutoffs <- diet |>
  summarise(
    q1 = quantile(mean_daily_energy_kcal, 0.25),
    q3 = quantile(mean_daily_energy_kcal, 0.75),
    iqr = IQR(mean_daily_energy_kcal),
    lower_cutoff = q1 - 1.5 * iqr,
    upper_cutoff = q3 + 1.5 * iqr
  )

energy_cutoffs
# A tibble: 1 × 5
     q1    q3   iqr lower_cutoff upper_cutoff
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>        <dbl>        <dbl>
1 1389. 2327.  938.        -18.9        3735.

Flag unusual values.

diet_flagged <- diet |>
  mutate(
    high_energy_outlier = mean_daily_energy_kcal > energy_cutoffs$upper_cutoff
  )

diet_flagged |>
  count(high_energy_outlier)
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  high_energy_outlier     n
  <lgl>               <int>
1 FALSE                6593
2 TRUE                  159

Visualize The Flag

p1 <- ggplot(
  diet_flagged,
  aes(
    x = mean_daily_energy_kcal,
    fill = high_energy_outlier
  )
) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 250)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "firebrick", "FALSE" = "gray70"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Not flagged", "TRUE" = "Flagged high"),
    name = NULL
  )

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  labs(
    title = "High Energy Intake Values Flagged By IQR Rule",
    x = "Mean daily energy intake, kcal",
    y = "Number of participants"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3

Inspect Flagged Rows

diet_flagged |>
  filter(high_energy_outlier) |>
  select(respondent_id, age_years, gender, age_group, dietary_days, mean_daily_energy_kcal) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_daily_energy_kcal)) |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 6
   respondent_id age_years gender age_group dietary_days mean_daily_energy_kcal
           <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>  <chr>            <dbl>                  <dbl>
 1        139174        27 male   18-29                1                 10446 
 2        135541        68 male   65-79                1                  8179 
 3        135173        58 male   45-64                2                  8135 
 4        138180        18 male   18-29                1                  7645 
 5        136473        60 male   45-64                2                  6977 
 6        130904        28 male   18-29                1                  6723 
 7        139856        35 male   30-44                1                  6657 
 8        131077        47 male   45-64                2                  6650.
 9        130801        56 male   45-64                2                  6502.
10        131498        41 female 30-44                2                  6306.

Compare With A Box Plot

p1 <- ggplot(
  diet,
  aes(x = age_group, y = mean_daily_energy_kcal)
) +
  geom_boxplot()

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Daily Energy Intake By Age Group",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Mean daily energy intake, kcal"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Takeaways

  • Outliers can be errors, true rare values, or signs that the variable needs context.
  • A rule like 1.5 IQR is a screening tool, not a final judgment.
  • Visualization helps show whether unusual values are isolated or part of a tail.
  • Inspect the rows behind surprising values before deciding what to do.
